items.mistake: Equating high correlation with high mutual information (or vice versa)
items.fix: Compute I(X; Y) directly or use MI-based feature scores when relationships may be non-linear; use correlation only when linear association is the explicit target.
items.why: A U-shaped relationship can have near-zero Pearson r while I(X; Y) is substantial because MI detects any dependence in the joint PMF.
items.example: Y = (X - 0.5)^2 with X uniform on [0,1]: correlation ≈ 0, but discretizing X into bins shows clear dependence and positive MI.

items.mistake: Treating estimated MI from small samples as exact
items.fix: Use more data, cross-validated MI estimates, or permutation tests; report confidence intervals or rank features by relative MI, not absolute thresholds alone.
items.why: Finite-sample MI estimators are biased upward for discrete counts and noisy for continuous k-NN methods—spurious high scores appear with tiny n.
items.example: With n=20 and a binary label, a random numeric feature can show I ≈ 0.15 bits by chance; repeat with n=5000 and the score collapses toward 0.

items.mistake: Confusing I(X; Y) with I(X; Y | Z) or conditional MI notation
items.fix: Write conditioning explicitly: I(X; Y | Z) measures shared information between X and Y given Z. Unconditional I(X; Y) integrates over Z.
items.why: Colliders and confounders change MI when conditioning; skipping the bar leads to wrong causal and feature-selection conclusions.
items.example: X and Y independent given Z but marginally dependent when Z is unobserved—I(X; Y) > 0 while I(X; Y | Z) = 0.
